title: Blood in Cat Urine (Hematuria): Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your cat has dark or bloody urine, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn why this happens and what steps you need to take right now.
content_html:
What is it?

Dark or bloody urine in cats, medically known as hematuria, is the presence of red blood cells in the urine. This can appear as pinkish fluid, bright red spots, or dark, tea-colored liquid. It is usually a symptom of severe inflammation, infection, or injury within the urinary tract, including the bladder or kidneys.

Is it an emergency?

The urgency level for this symptom is High. You should contact a veterinarian immediately. While it can be caused by a simple infection, it is often a sign of Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease (FLUTD) or a life-threatening urinary blockage. In male cats, a blockage can become fatal within 24 to 48 hours if the cat is unable to pass urine. If your cat is straining, crying, or repeatedly visiting the litter box, seek emergency care now.

Why a photo helps triage

If you discover bloody urine, taking a clear photo can be an invaluable tool for your vet. A photo helps the medical team see the exact color, the presence of blood clots, and the volume of urine passed. This visual evidence helps them determine the severity of the irritation and prioritize your cat's care during the triage process.

faqs.question: Can I wait until tomorrow to see my regular vet?
faqs.answer: Because urinary issues can escalate to a total blockage very quickly, it is not recommended to wait. If your cat cannot urinate at all, it is a critical emergency.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms to look for?
faqs.answer: Watch for straining in the litter box, vocalizing (crying) while peeing, excessive grooming of the genital area, or urinating in unusual places like the sink or bathtub.

faqs.question: Is blood in urine common in cats?
faqs.answer: It is a common sign of stress-induced cystitis or bladder stones, but it is never considered 'normal' and always requires a veterinary evaluation to rule out life-threatening issues.
